title: Antenatal Visit: Danger Signs Screening
short_description: Learn how to handle Antenatal Visit: Danger Signs Screening correctly in the field.

questions.patient_prompt: You are completing a routine 7th-month ANC follow up. Sita mentions she has been having 'very bad headaches' since yesterday and her vision is sometimes blurry. What are you most concerned about?
questions.mcq_question: What is the most accurate clinical assessment for these symptoms?

options.text: These are common pregnancy symptoms caused by stress.
These are severe danger signs of Pre-eclampsia.
She is likely just dehydrated and needs ORS.
She needs to lie down and take a paracetamol.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Severe headache combined with blurred vision in the third trimester are classic danger signs of Pre-eclampsia.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Severe headache and blurred vision are critical danger signs for Pre-eclampsia.

questions.patient_prompt: After identifying the danger signs, Sita asks if she can just rest until her husband comes back tomorrow evening. What should you do?
questions.mcq_question: What is your immediate next action?

options.text: Agree, and tell her to call you if the headache gets worse tomorrow.
Immediately arrange transport and refer her to the nearest CHC/FRU.
Tell her to walk to the sub-center immediately to check BP.
Give her an iron-folic acid tablet to help with the headache.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Pre-eclampsia can escalate to severe eclampsia (seizures) very quickly. Immediate referral is needed.
questions.explanation_wrong: Wrong. You missed a critical referral. You must never delay care when eclampsia danger signs are present.

questions.patient_prompt: While arranging the transport, Sita tells you she hasn't felt the baby move at all today. How do you respond?
questions.mcq_question: Which of these is the correct protocol response to decreased fetal movement?

options.text: Tell her babies sleep a lot inside and not to worry.
Tell her to drink a sugary drink and wait an hour.
Acknowledge this as another critical danger sign (Decreased Fetal Movement) and expedite the referral.
Press hard on her stomach to wake the baby up.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Decreased fetal movement is another major danger sign indicating potential fetal distress.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Decreased fetal movement is a major danger sign. Dismissing it delays life-saving care.

questions.patient_prompt: Sita is admitted and treated. Before you leave the hospital, what documentation must you complete?
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct tracking protocol?

options.text: Update the MCP card and your ASHA diary with the referral details and diagnosis.
Nothing, the hospital does all the paperwork.
Just tell the ANM verbally next month.
Fill out a birth certificate in advance.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Maintaining the Mother and Child Protection (MCP) card and your own ASHA diary is crucial.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Proper documentation in the MCP card and ASHA diary is a mandatory protocol step.

title: Postnatal Follow-up: Heavy Bleeding
short_description: Manage a postnatal emergency of postpartum hemorrhage.

questions.patient_prompt: You visit Maya on Day 3 after delivery. She says she is changing her pad every hour because it's completely soaked.
questions.mcq_question: What does this symptom indicate?

options.text: Normal lochia flow after childbirth.
Postpartum Hemorrhage (PPH) - a severe danger sign.
A minor infection that needs antibiotics.
Dehydration.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Soaking a pad every hour is classic Postpartum Hemorrhage (PPH) and is a massive medical emergency.
questions.explanation_wrong: Wrong. This is highly abnormal and life-threatening bleeding (PPH).

questions.patient_prompt: Maya's mother-in-law says she will give her hot turmeric milk to stop the bleeding. What is your action?
questions.mcq_question: What is the immediate ASHA protocol?

options.text: Agree and wait 2 hours to see if it works.
Tell her to sleep with legs elevated.
Call an ambulance (108) immediately for emergency referral.
Give her IFA tablets to quickly replace the blood loss.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. PPH requires immediate hospitalization; any delay can result in maternal death.
questions.explanation_wrong: Wrong! You cannot treat active hemorrhaging at home. You MUST call an ambulance immediately.

questions.patient_prompt: While waiting for the ambulance, Maya feels dizzy and her skin is pale and cold.
questions.mcq_question: What should you do while waiting for transport?

options.text: Keep her warm, elevate her legs, and encourage ORS if she is conscious.
Make her walk around to keep her awake.
Give her a strong cup of tea.
Start chest compressions.

questions.explanation_correct: Shock management protocol involves keeping the mother warm and elevating legs to direct blood to critical organs.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. You must treat for hemorrhagic shock by keeping her flat, legs elevated, and warm.

title: Child Diarrhea: ORS + Zinc
short_description: Treating acute childhood diarrhea and preventing dehydration.

questions.patient_prompt: A mother brings her 2-year-old son who has had 5 watery stools today. He is thirsty and restless.
questions.mcq_question: Which classification of dehydration does this child have based on IMNCI guidelines?

options.text: No Dehydration
Some Dehydration
Severe Dehydration
Chronic Diarrhea

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Being restless and thirsty are key indicators of 'Some Dehydration' requiring Plan B treatment.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Thirst and restlessness indicate 'Some Dehydration'.

questions.patient_prompt: How will you instruct the mother to prepare ORS?
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct protocol for ORS mixing?

options.text: Mix half the packet in 1 glass of water.
Mix one full packet in 1 Litre of clean drinking water.
Boil the ORS powder with milk.
Mix 2 spoons in a small bowl of water.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! The WHO standard protocol is one entire packet in 1 Litre of water.
questions.explanation_wrong: Wrong. ORS must always be mixed entirely in exactly 1 Litre of water to prevent electrolyte imbalance.

questions.patient_prompt: The mother asks if she should stop giving the baby food until the diarrhea stops.
questions.mcq_question: What is your counseling response?

options.text: Yes, rest the stomach for 24 hours.
No, continue feeding and giving breastmilk to prevent malnutrition.
Only give water with salt.
Give only solid dry foods.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Starving a child during diarrhea worsens malnutrition. Feeding must continue.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. You must ALWAYS instruct the mother to continue feeding and breastfeeding during diarrhea.

title: Child Fever: Danger Signs
short_description: Assess a febrile child for malaria, pneumonia, and other severe diseases.

questions.patient_prompt: A 3-year-old child has had a high fever for 3 days. When you examine the child, you notice fast breathing (50 breaths per minute).
questions.mcq_question: What does fast breathing alongside fever indicate?

options.text: Pneumonia
Simple Cold
Malaria
Measles

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Fast breathing (>40 breaths/min for a 3-year-old) is the primary IMNCI criteria for Pneumonia.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Fast breathing is the cardinal sign of Pneumonia in children.

questions.patient_prompt: You also note lower chest wall in-drawing when the child breathes in.
questions.mcq_question: What is your clinical decision?

options.text: Give paracetamol and check tomorrow.
Refer URGENTLY to a hospital as this is Severe Pneumonia.
Give ORS.
Do a Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT).

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Chest in-drawing means Severe Pneumonia, which requires urgently injected antibiotics at a facility.
questions.explanation_wrong: Wrong! Chest in-drawing is a massive danger sign of Severe Pneumonia. Immediate referral is mandatory.

title: TB Suspect: Cough > 2 Weeks
short_description: Roleplay identifying and referring a suspected Tuberculosis patient.

questions.patient_prompt: Ramesh, a 45-year-old man, complains of a constant cough that hasn't gone away for 3 weeks. He also mentions night sweats.
questions.mcq_question: According to NTEP guidelines, what should be your immediate suspicion?

options.text: Seasonal Flu
Asthma
Tuberculosis (TB) Suspect
COVID-19 definitely

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Cough > 2 weeks accompanied by night sweats classifies him as a presumptive TB case.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Any cough lasting longer than 2 weeks MUST be treated as a TB suspect according to national guidelines.

questions.patient_prompt: Ramesh refuses to go to the hospital because he thinks they will charge him a lot of money.
questions.mcq_question: How do you counsel him?

options.text: Tell him TB testing and treatment (DOTS) is completely free at government hospitals.
Offer him some herbal cough syrup.
Tell him to go to a private clinic.
Tell him to ignore it, it's just a cough.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. A key ASHA responsibility is educating patients that TB diagnosis and medication are 100% free under the government.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. You must inform him that government TB services are completely free to encourage testing.

questions.patient_prompt: What specimen needs to be collected for his TB diagnostic test?
questions.mcq_question: What test is ordered for a presumptive pulmonary TB patient?

options.text: Blood Test
Sputum Test (Two samples)
Urine Test
Throat Swab

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Two sputum samples (one on the spot, one early morning) are required for an Acid-Fast Bacilli (AFB) smear or CBNAAT.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Pulmonary TB requires a Sputum Test to identify the mycobacterium.

title: Diabetes Follow-up: Adherence
short_description: Conducting a home visit for a known Diabetic patient.

questions.patient_prompt: You are visiting Geeta, who has Type 2 Diabetes. She says she stopped her medication last week because she 'felt fine'.
questions.mcq_question: What is your response?

options.text: Agree with her. Only take medicine when feeling sick.
Counsel her that Diabetes is a lifelong condition and medication must never be stopped without consulting a doctor, even if feeling well.
Give her double the dose today.
Tell her to just avoid sugar instead of medicine.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. ASHA workers play a critical role in ensuring NCD treatment adherence, as patients often incorrectly stop meds when asymptomatic.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Diabetes medication must be taken daily as prescribed. Stopping it causes dangerous silent sugar spikes.

questions.patient_prompt: Geeta mentions she has a small cut on the sole of her foot that hasn't healed in a month.
questions.mcq_question: What should you do?

options.text: Tell her to wash it with soap.
Give her a band-aid.
Recognize this as a high-risk 'Diabetic Foot Ulcer' and refer her immediately to the PHC/Doctor.
Apply some hot oil to it.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Non-healing wounds in diabetics can quickly lead to gangrene and amputation. Immediate referral is needed.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. A non-healing wound in a diabetic is a massive red flag requiring a doctor's intervention.

questions.patient_prompt: An elderly man complains of a severe, throbbing headache, chest tightness, and dizziness. He is a known hypertensive.
questions.mcq_question: What is your clinical interpretation?

options.text: He just needs to sleep.
He is likely experiencing a Hypertensive Crisis, a medical emergency.
He has a cold.
He needs a massage.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Severe headache and chest pain in a known hypertensive indicate dangerously high blood pressure.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. These are critical danger signs for stroke or heart attack induced by high BP.

questions.patient_prompt: You check his BP using your digital monitor. It reads 190/110 mmHg.
questions.mcq_question: What must you do?

options.text: Urgent referral to the nearest hospital.
Give him salt water.
Wait 5 hours and check again.
Give him paracetamol.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. BP over 180/110 is a crisis and requires immediate injected anti-hypertensives at a facility.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Blood pressure this high can cause an immediate brain hemorrhage. Rapid referral is required.

title: Child Immunization: Hesitancy
short_description: Counsel a family refusing the Pentavalent vaccine.

questions.patient_prompt: A mother refuses to give her 6-week-old baby the Pentavalent vaccine because 'the last time the older child got a shot, he had a fever for two days'.
questions.mcq_question: How do you respond?

options.text: Tell her vaccines are bad anyway.
Scold her for being uneducated.
Empathize, explain that mild fever is a normal sign the medicine is working, and the vaccine protects from 5 deadly diseases.
Force her to bring the child.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Effective ASHA counseling relies on empathy, addressing fears, and explaining the immense benefits of the vaccine.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Scolding or forcing ruins trust. You must respectfully explain that mild fever is normal.

title: Newborn: Not Feeding
short_description: Assess a neonate during an HBNC visit.

questions.patient_prompt: On Day 3 of HBNC (Home Based Newborn Care), you find the newborn is completely lethargic and refusing to breastfeed.
questions.mcq_question: How does the IMNCI protocol classify 'Not feeding since birth' or 'stopped feeding well'?

options.text: A normal phase for newborns.
A Severe Danger Sign requiring immediate referral.
A sign the mother's milk is bad.
A sign the baby needs formula milk.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. A neonate refusing to feed is one of the absolute most critical danger signs indicating sepsis or hypothermia.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Not feeding is a SEVERE danger sign in newborns. You must refer immediately.

questions.patient_prompt: You measure a 2-year-old child's Mid-Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC). The tape shows Red.
questions.mcq_question: What does the Red zone on the MUAC tape indicate?

options.text: Normal nutrition
Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM)
Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM)
Obesity

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Red (< 11.5 cm) indicates Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM), meaning the child is at high risk of death.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Red indicates Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM).

questions.patient_prompt: What is the standard protocol for a child identified as SAM with medical complications (like edema or fever)?
questions.mcq_question: Where should the child be sent?

options.text: Refer to the Anganwadi center for extra rations only.
Admit to the nearest Nutritional Rehabilitation Centre (NRC).
Tell the mother to feed the child more rice.
Give iron syrup and wait.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. SAM children with complications require inpatient medical and nutritional therapy at an NRC.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. SAM is a medical emergency requiring admission to a Nutritional Rehabilitation Centre (NRC).

questions.patient_prompt: A young mother wants to space her next child but believes Copper-T (IUD) will travel to her heart.
questions.mcq_question: How do you counsel her?

options.text: Tell her it's true and offer pills instead.
Laugh at her for believing a myth.
Respectfully bust the myth using anatomical flip-charts, explaining it stays safely in the uterus.
Tell her her husband should decide.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Using IEC materials (flip charts) to visually bust myths is the best way to encourage modern contraceptive adoption.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Empathy and visual education are required. Never validate a myth.

questions.patient_prompt: During a PNC visit (Day 14), you notice the new mother is constantly crying, says she feels worthless, and refuses to hold the baby.
questions.mcq_question: What is your assessment?

options.text: She is just lazy.
Signs of severe Postpartum Depression (PPD).
Normal behavior for new mothers.
She just needs more sleep.

questions.explanation_correct: Correct. Feelings of worthlessness and rejecting the baby are huge red flags for Postpartum Depression requiring psychiatric support.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. These are serious signs of clinical Postpartum Depression, not normal fatigue.
